channel.title: Destructuring on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Destructuring on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] Kotlin Destructuring Component Error Fix
item.description:
What This Error Means

A destructuring component error occurs when the componentN() functions are not properly defined for an object. These functions are required for destructuring declarations.

Common Causes

  • Missing component functions
  • Wrong component function signatures
  • Components return wrong types
  • Not using operator keyword

How to Fix

// WRONG: Missing component functions
class User(val name: String, val age: Int)
val (name, age) = User("Alice", 30) // Error

// CORRECT: Add component functions
class User(val name: String, val age: Int) {
 operator fun component1() = name
 operator fun component2() = age
}
val (name, age) = User("Alice", 30)

// WRONG: Wrong component count
data class Triple(val a: Int, val b: Int, val c: Int)
val (x, y) = Triple(1, 2, 3) // OK but ignores c

// CORRECT: Match component count
val (x, y, z) = Triple(1, 2, 3)

Examples

// Example 1: Manual component functions
class Range(val start: Int, val end: Int) {
 operator fun component1() = start
 operator fun component2() = end
}
val (start, end) = Range(1, 10)

// Example 2: Extension component functions
class User(val name: String, val age: Int)
operator fun User.component1() = name
operator fun User.component2() = age

val (name, age) = User("Alice", 30)

// Example 3: Pair and Triple
val pair = Pair("hello", 42)
val (str, num) = pair

val triple = Triple(1, 2, 3)
val (a, b, c) = triple

Related Errors

  • Destructuring declaration error — destructuring issue
  • Destructuring iterator error — iterator issue
  • ClassCastException — type cast failed

item.title: [Solution] Kotlin Destructuring Declaration Error Fix
item.description:
What This Error Means

A destructuring declaration error occurs when you try to destructure an object that does not have the required componentN() functions. Data classes provide these automatically, but custom classes need manual implementation.

Common Causes

  • Object missing component functions
  • Wrong number of variables
  • Not a data class
  • Missing componentN functions

How to Fix

// WRONG: Non-data class destructuring
class User(val name: String, val age: Int)
val (name, age) = User("Alice", 30) // Error: no component functions

// CORRECT: Use data class
data class User(val name: String, val age: Int)
val (name, age) = User("Alice", 30)

// WRONG: Wrong number of variables
data class User(val name: String, val age: Int)
val (name) = User("Alice", 30) // OK but ignores age
val (name, age, email) = User("Alice", 30) // Error: not enough components

// CORRECT: Match number of variables
data class User(val name: String, val age: Int)
val (name, age) = User("Alice", 30)

Examples

// Example 1: Data class destructuring
data class Point(val x: Int, val y: Int)
val (x, y) = Point(1, 2)
println("x=$x, y=$y")

// Example 2: Map destructuring
val map = mapOf("a" to 1, "b" to 2)
for ((key, value) in map) {
 println("$key: $value")
}

// Example 3: Custom component functions
class User(val name: String, val age: Int) {
 operator fun component1() = name
 operator fun component2() = age
}
val (name, age) = User("Alice", 30)

Related Errors

  • Destructuring component error — component issue
  • Destructuring iterator error — iterator issue
  • ClassCastException — type cast failed

item.title: [Solution] Kotlin Destructuring Iterator Error Fix
item.description:
What This Error Means

A destructuring iterator error occurs when iterating over a collection with destructuring and the iterator does not provide the expected number of values.

Common Causes

  • Iterator does not support destructuring
  • Wrong number of values from iterator
  • Missing iterator function
  • Wrong collection type

How to Fix

// WRONG: Iterator without destructuring support
class SimpleList(val items: List<Int>) {
 fun iterator() = items.iterator()
}
val list = SimpleList(listOf(1, 2, 3))
for ((index, value) in list) { // Error: iterator returns Int, not Pair
 println("$index: $value")
}

// CORRECT: Use indexed access
val list = SimpleList(listOf(1, 2, 3))
for ((index, value) in list.items.withIndex()) {
 println("$index: $value")
}

// WRONG: Wrong number of destructuring variables
val pairs = listOf(Pair("a", 1), Pair("b", 2))
for ((key, value, extra) in pairs) { // Error: only 2 components
 println("$key: $value")
}

// CORRECT: Match component count
for ((key, value) in pairs) {
 println("$key: $value")
}

Examples

// Example 1: withIndex
val list = listOf("a", "b", "c")
for ((index, value) in list.withIndex()) {
 println("$index: $value")
}

// Example 2: Map destructuring
val map = mapOf("a" to 1, "b" to 2)
for ((key, value) in map) {
 println("$key: $value")
}

// Example 3: Custom iterator
class Counter(val max: Int) : Iterator<Int> {
 private var current = 0
 override fun hasNext() = current < max
 override fun next() = current++
}
for (i in Counter(5)) {
 println(i)
}

Related Errors

  • Destructuring declaration error — destructuring issue
  • Destructuring component error — component issue
  • IndexOutOfBoundsException — index out of range

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2356 — Type 'X' has no properties in common with type 'Y'
item.description:
TS2356 — Type ‘X’ has no properties in common with type ‘Y’

TS2356 occurs when you compare or assign two types that share no common properties. TypeScript’s structural type system finds zero overlap between the types.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Comparing unrelated types
interface Cat {
 purr(): void;
}
interface Car {
 drive(): void;
}

const cat: Cat = { purr() {} };
const car: Car = { drive() {} };

if (cat === car) { // TS2356
 console.log("same");
}

// Cause 2: Assigning incompatible types
const cat2: Cat = car; // TS2356

// Cause 3: Union with no overlap
type Result = string & number; // TS2356

How to Fix

Fix 1: Don’t compare incompatible types

// Remove the comparison or use a common type
interface Pet {
 name: string;
}
interface Cat extends Pet { purr(): void; }
interface Dog extends Pet { bark(): void; }

Fix 2: Use type guard or discriminator

function isCat(val: Cat | Car): val is Cat {
 return "purr" in val;
}

Fix 3: Fix the union type

type Result = string | number; // use union, not intersection

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type not assignable — general type mismatch.
  • TS2367: Comparison unintentional — impossible comparison.
  • TS2345: Argument not assignable — argument type mismatch.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS7031 — Binding Element Implicitly Has 'any' Type
item.description:
TS7031 — Binding element ‘X’ implicitly has an ‘any’ type

TS7031 occurs when destructured parameters in a function don’t have type annotations and noImplicitAny is enabled. This is similar to TS7006 but specifically targets destructured binding elements.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Destructured function parameter without types
function greet({ name, age }) { // TS7031: 'name' implicitly has 'any' type
 return `Hello ${name}, you are ${age}`;
}

// Cause 2: Destructured in arrow functions
const process = ({ id, label }) => { // TS7031
 console.log(id, label);
};

// Cause 3: Destructured in method parameters
class Service {
 handle({ data, status }) { // TS7031
 return data;
 }
}

// Cause 4: Destructured with defaults but no type
function configure({ host = "localhost", port = 3000 }) { // TS7031
 console.log(host, port);
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Annotate the destructured parameter with an interface

interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}

// Wrong
function greet({ name, age }) {}

// Correct
function greet({ name, age }: User) {}

Fix 2: Use inline type annotations

// Wrong
function greet({ name, age }) {}

// Correct
function greet({ name, age }: { name: string; age: number }) {}

Fix 3: Type destructured defaults

// Wrong
function configure({ host = "localhost", port = 3000 }) {}

// Correct
function configure({ host = "localhost", port = 3000 }: { host?: string; port?: number }) {}

Fix 4: Type arrow function parameters

// Wrong
const process = ({ id, label }) => {};

// Correct
const process = ({ id, label }: { id: number; label: string }) => {};

Examples

// Example 1: React component props
function Button({ onClick, label, disabled }) { // TS7031
 return <button onClick={onClick} disabled={disabled}>{label}</button>;
}

// Fix: type the props
interface ButtonProps {
 onClick: () => void;
 label: string;
 disabled?: boolean;
}
function Button({ onClick, label, disabled }: ButtonProps) {}

Related Errors

  • TS7006: Parameter implicitly has ‘any’ type — non-destructured parameters.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — related to object shape.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — missing required properties.
